text:
## First programming experiences (1967, high school)

**Adam:** Okay, so give me your take on your background.

**TQ:** Okay, here is the overview of my life and career. I wrote my first computer program in 1967. On a Wang calculator. Then on an IBM-something-who-the-hell-knows-what-it-was in the computer club at school using punch cards.

Then I got involved in a program done by IBM and SRI, supposedly about education using computers. They took a classroom and they put a couple of teletypes — paper, but they were IBM Selectric-based teletypes, so they were cool. They gave us each a booklet with an academic topic and never mentioned it again. But they gave us access to these computers. And the computers were interpreters. When you typed into them, you were typing a computer programming language called APL. APL is one of the seminal computer languages.

They never mentioned the academic topics again, ever. What I think was actually going on is that the people back at IBM were looking at what we typed and what we did. And what I did is I wrote programs. The one that I remember was I wrote Baccarat. You could play Baccarat against the computer with it typing out diagrams. It was a reasonably complex thing for a completely untrained kid.

**Adam:** How old were you at this point?

**TQ:** Junior in high school, I guess. All of this was happening in the '67-ish range. And that's where I started being a computer programmer.
label: First programming experiences (1967, high school)

text:
## College and pivot to engineering degree at Berkeley (~1975–1978)

**TQ:** Time goes on. I go to college and my family fell apart in fairly brutal ways and so I was largely on my own, albeit having a rich father. And so I ended up, after spending a couple of years being an exquisitely drug-addled hippie and having a great time, I went to college where I did liberal arts. I studied philosophy, and basically had myself on a path to being some kind of liberal arts consequence. When my dad said: "Get a degree!"

And at this point I was living in California, in Mill Valley, and the intention was to go to Berkeley. I applied to Berkeley and they said, "Oh, you are going to have to take English 101 when you get here." And I had assumed that I would not because I had like two and a half years of all English-oriented stuff in place. I came to the understanding that my father would murder me. And it turns out that back in these years, '75 or whatever, they didn't think that engineers and scientists needed to be able to speak English, and they would allow me to get a degree in electrical engineering and computer science without having to take English again.

I had not really given any serious thought to being a computer programmer. But I remembered that I liked it. And so with that much planning and intentionality, I set off to become an engineer. I have a degree in electrical engineering and computer science. And I did that, and it worked.
label: College and engineering degree at Berkeley (~1975–1978)

text:
## Hired at R.R. Donnelley & Sons, pioneering digital imaging (1979–early 1980s)

**TQ:** So I get out of college and I was planning on going to work somewhere in what would become the Silicon Valley. But I went home for Christmas vacation — I graduated in December of '78 — and my dad's nose was out of joint that I wasn't even considering working in Chicago. And it turns out his wife, a hot young Sicilian woman, had a friend who was a headhunter.

I agreed to go on some interviews, of which I actually went on one. It was at R.R. Donnelley & Sons, the gigantic international printing company. And they had a project they were hiring for that we ended up calling Filmless Preliminary. That is to say: no film.

Back in the day, there was a thing called color separation, where they used filters and cameras to separate the reds, greens, and blues for use with cyan, magenta, and yellow ink to produce printed pieces. This is film that they then contact-printed onto printing plates.

Some smarty pants got the idea that with computers you should be able to digitize the color separations. This was novel stuff. And I got hired into this project.

It was an unbelievable experience. By the time it was done, I was literally a world-class expert in digital imaging technology.

**Adam:** Why were you a world-class expert?

**TQ:** Because we invented digital imaging. When I was hired, there were two other companies — three within a short period of time — working on this tech, totaling maybe a few dozen people, and I was one of them. I wrote the first file format that was used for exchanging images among computer systems.

My team created the first imaging system — a color separation scanner that put pictures on a computer disk. The first one in the world.

My personal main project, two things. I have a patent for color correction technology — a program that I wrote in assembly language that did six-dimensional color manipulation for digital images.

**Adam:** Crazy stuff.

**TQ:** Hexadecimal, baby. And another of my big projects — I was the project manager for a digital image manipulation terminal. Each terminal cost a half a million dollars and did about a quarter of a percent of what Photoshop does. But at the time, it was the beginning of people being able to see pictures on a computer screen, and I was one of the people that created the very first instance of that.

The system we made, they ended up spending 25 million bucks on equipment to implement our project in three different places in the country. This is 25 million 1980 dollars — so 100 million or 150 million today.
label: Hired at R.R. Donnelley & Sons, pioneering digital imaging (1979–early 1980s)

text:
## Network design and production data management at Donnelley (early 1980s)

**TQ:** And one of my main personal skills is systems thinking.

**Adam:** That's exactly the phrase that I've been thinking while you've been talking.

**TQ:** I became responsible for designing the computer networks. We didn't have networking networks back then. What we did is we had great big disk drives that had two ports on them — so you could plug two computers into them. These disk drives were literally the size of a washing machine. And they held a whopping 300 megabytes. And they cost 40 grand each in 1980 dollars.

We had these big production systems and we needed to be able to move pictures through them — the color separation stage, the printing plate stage, the manipulation terminals, proofing prints. I did these diagrams. I designed these network systems and everybody respected my talent for it. I drew these beautiful diagrams, of which I wish I had one, because one of the things is they had to be pretty. If they weren't pretty, they weren't going to work. I just knew that.

That caused me to be involved in data communication, which caused me to become aware of Ethernet. And also made me aware of the problems of managing files. I ended up doing a project for production data management. I actually had a phone conversation with Bob Metcalfe — he wanted me to buy his Ethernet card, but his was a shared memory card and I preferred direct memory access, or the other way around.

I designed a production data management system with a database. Back then databases were new — SQL was invented in 1970, and this is 1983. I ended up doing production data management, networks, early networking. I also participated in probably the first example of satellite network communication, working out the protocols for moving images between our production installations.
label: Network design and production data management at Donnelley (early 1980s)

text:
## Donnelley consulting, early AI work, and departure (~1983–1986)

**TQ:** I ended up being co-opted into a team that was consulting for our customers to help them get futuristic. Family Circle Magazine — at the time the biggest circulation magazine in the country — JCPenney, Scientific American, are all places that I worked.

One of the things I did for JCPenney — I designed a catalog production system for them. We needed to have a terminal that did design kind of stuff, more like Illustrator than Photoshop. At this point laser writers were out, so we knew about PostScript. I figured PostScript would be a cool thing to use for this.

I also knew Chuck Geschke and John Warnock, who were the guys who invented PostScript. I was at Adobe Systems when Adobe Systems was in a 1,500 square foot office in an industrial strip in Palo Alto or someplace. John Warnock sitting in his office showing me his prototype for Adobe Illustrator.

**Adam:** This is nuts.

**TQ:** And here's the last of the stories from this era. I end up doing presentations at Fortune 500 companies. Consulting with these big companies doing all this high-level stuff. And I end up having dinner with Marvin Minsky — the grandfather of artificial intelligence. He was a very nice guy, very interesting. This was the '85-ish or so, '86.

And neural networks were a thing. We had a process for laying pages out on great big printing sheets that I came to understand was perfect for neural networks. There were domain experts �� people that knew how to do it — but the experts were old and retiring and there weren't new ones.

Oh my God, it just made me realize another project I worked on. I participated in an early MIT Media Lab project, where I designed something I called the "intelligent application."

It actually is the reason I left Donnelley — I put together this fabulous project. It was actually two things. And they wouldn't do them. I was absolutely furious.

Then this Neural Networks thing came along. There was a company called Symbolics, which made an object-oriented Unix machine specifically intended for artificial intelligence research. I ended up convincing Donnelley to pay for me to spend three weeks in Sunnyvale on the Stanford campus learning about these machines. Also seminal — changed the way I looked at software development forever.

I had put together and got approval to buy one of these machines and to work towards replacing the imposition guys with artificial intelligence. And some vice-president redirected the money away from me.

First there was the Intelligent Publication thing — I worked on that for two years, a great idea with so many benefits for the company. And now this, which also has huge, concrete, direct benefits. And no, we're not doing that either. And by the way, we're not doing that in a nasty political way.

And I quit.
label: Donnelley consulting, early AI work, and departure (~1983–1986)

text:
## Co-founded color separation business (~1986–1988)

**TQ:** So there's a ton of background experiences that are very high-level, very cool. I spent a little time idle and then went to work with a friend from Donnelley. He and me and another guy had a color separation company. We did color separations the old-fashioned way with multi-hundred-thousand-dollar equipment that wrote color separations on film. And we did something called stripping — combining color separation films with type films to make printing plates.

I was the administrative guy who wrote software to run the business. My old partner was still using the software I wrote to run his business 18 years later. And he was doing digital printing, inkjet stuff.

I did that for two and a half years or something. And it was great when it was great. But there came a time when we'd get a big project in and instead of looking and going "oh, 30,000 bucks," I'd go "oh shit, I have to do this again." And I quit.
label: Co-founded color separation business (~1986–1988)

text:
## Hired at SciTex as technical sales support (~1987–1993)

**TQ:** I was still an expert. I was well known. I had done presentations everywhere. And I got hired — first as consultant then full-time — by a company that sold computer equipment to the printing business, one of those three companies I talked about. I was a technical sales support guy — the god of technical sales support. The way I looked at it: I had 13 secretaries. They called themselves salespeople, making appointments for me to go and figure out how to sell to people.

As my experience — my time working at Donnelley as a consultant to their giant customers — made me an experienced interactor with all levels of management. And my time at Donnelley R&D made me both extraordinarily knowledgeable about digital printing topics and prepared me to be a very thorough analyst. Also, I have the knack. Some people can play the piano. I can analyze and think about systems.

I had a great time doing that. I traveled all over the place. I worked with a sales guy to build a national accounts sales organization and led that technical sales support effort. And by leading it, mostly, I did it. We had other minions, but if it was anything important, I did it.

It was a fabulous experience. It gave me all sorts of organizational learnings along the way.
label: Hired at SciTex as technical sales support (~1987–1993)

text:
## TQ leads national tractor trailer demo tour at SciTex

**TQ:** It makes me think of another anecdote from the beginning of my SciTex stuff. My father was an artist. He designed grocery store packaging. And he was a big shot. And I worked for him for a few years.

Because of that, at SciTex, they built a digital workstation specifically for the package business. And I got recruited to be the technical sales support for it. They got a tractor trailer and built it into a demonstration studio and dragged it around the country, where I would fly and meet it and spend two or three days demonstrating to the prospects in that neighborhood.

And because I did the demonstration so often, I got incredibly good at it. I could fully interact with the people and keep the demonstration completely on track. I became famous and beloved. I would walk out of the trailer and literally sales guys would run up to me and rub my shoulders and pray and all kinds of stuff. It was so fulfilling and so fun.
label: SciTex national tractor trailer demo tour

text:
## TQ conducts demo with no cables at SciTex trade show

**TQ:** And I went to a trade show one time and I was by myself. It was some marginal thing. I get there and the equipment is there ��� monitor, computer, printer. The boxes were there. There was not a single cable. Not even a power cable. It was a very significant error on the part of whoever did the logistics.

Then it's nine o'clock and the show opens. There I am with no cables. I'm like, okay, shit. What I did is I set up my booth and I gave the demonstrations that were scheduled. I said: "I got no cables. But if I did have cables, what you could see here..." — and I'd point to the screen — and because I was so insanely well-versed in it, I was able to do absolutely functional demos that people were fully engaged with, pointing at a black screen.

**Adam:** And I bet when you pointed at it, everybody would look at it, too.

**TQ:** Oh, they totally did. They totally imagined what I was telling them. I mean, it worked. It was wild. And of course, it is another moment when I walked away deeply impressed with my own skills, and thrilled at my ability. There is nothing that a person loves more than an opportunity to exert their mastery. And this was a time when I was on the top of my game. 45 years old, and I'm just absolutely kicking ass.
label: SciTex trade show demo with no cables

text:
## TQ leaves SciTex and joins Krauss America startup (~1996)

**TQ:** I left SciTex. I was there for six years and got tired of it. The company was starting to spin off its sales efforts to distributors. The writing was on the wall. I went with one of the sales guys to a distributor and worked there for a very short period of time. Then the guy with whom I did the national sales accounts thing — Terry — hits me up and says: "I'm working with a startup, and I want you to come with me."

The startup was a German company — a big, established German company called Krauss America. They had a direct digital printing-to-plate system. At this time, '96 and '97, this was unheard of. This did not exist at all.

I was one of a half a dozen people working for an incredibly hot pair of women who were banging the leader of the German government. I never came to a strong conclusion if this was a corporate initiative or a gift to the hot chick.

Anyhow, I did that, and it was great because the technology was new and the song and dance was not yet written. I was heavily respected from the first minute. I had a good time helping them try to sell it and build a distributor network.

I had 11-by-17 laminated cards that I made — what I would have normally put on a transparency slide viewer for a presentation. And I would go places with these cards and do the presentation holding up the slides. It was a much more personal, engaging, persuasive interaction than the usual audience-presenter distance.
label: Joins Krauss America startup (~1996)

text:
## TQ directs Krauss America's $350,000 Expo 97 trade show

**TQ:** We did a trade show. And it was terrible. Nothing about the corporate performance was good. And because I am who I am, I went home and I wrote a plan — a strategy plan, a whole thing about what we should do.

I was, all of this time, a full-size, masterful communicator in every medium known to humankind.

And I wrote a thorough, actionable, full-size plan for the next trade show. In particular was the big one: Expo 97 in Chicago. I had an innovative concept for it. They ended up spending $350,000 and had me direct it entirely.

We had a booth-building company. We had a big booth that had an actual stage with seating area. I had a demonstration production system built. I conceived this whole thing. We wrote a song to the tune of "Walk on the Wild Side" entitled "Walk on the Workflow."

We had two guys that did a comedic presentation explaining this thing, which I also wrote. I found the technical system — we gotta prototype this thing. I recruited one of our distributors in St. Louis, got all of the equipment shipped there, went there and spent a week, got all these people, and we built the system and made it work.

But it turns out my family had for two years been planning a group vacation — my in-laws, their kids, spouses, all of the folks and us were supposed to go to California. This vacation was scheduled long ahead. And it takes place in the two weeks leading up to the show.

The company's like: "Well, you can't do it, you can't have the time off." Like, okay, quit. This is my entire family. I'm not about to sacrifice my entire family for you.

Terry — who was a good friend and had been with me for seven or eight years and had seen me pull off miracle after miracle — he got with them and said: "Do not do this. TQ will make it work."

So they relented. And I did it. And it turned out to be actually a significant benefit, because everybody knew there was an actual hard deadline two weeks before the show. They weren't going to get more decisions from me, more leadership. As a consequence, everybody was ready two weeks before the show.

Instead of the usual scramble for the first half-day, they spent two weeks cleaning up the stuff that wasn't really ready. It all worked. The comedy show worked. The live processing with early-stage digital cameras and inkjet printers worked.

I got us significant strategic partnerships. I got us on the front page of the show daily. I did all of this stuff. It was one of the great accomplishments of my career — of my life, really. I gave a presentation to one of the daily keynotes to 600 people. I recruited a staff of 70 people to work the booth.

Again: who the hell was this? Where did this guy come from?

We get out of it, and I'm told that they had intended to fire me. Because they were still pissed that I went on vacation.

**Adam:** So they were going to fire you?

**TQ:** Yeah. But they didn't. They did kick me out a couple months later. I never really understood why, but I suspect it's because they had decided they didn't really want to do it. The American subsidiary dissolved like six or eight weeks after that.
label: Directs Krauss America's $350,000 Expo 97 trade show

text:
## Early blogging and marketing work at Krauss America (1996) and departure

**TQ:** But that was the place where I ran a blog in the 90s. Along the way, the internet appears, and I made them a website. It had, prominently on it, a column with notes about things.

Another thing I did — we bought magazine ads in trade publications. The strategy I came up with: they would buy the ad across from the editorial page. The editorial was two columns out of three, full page. We bought the column on the outside. And every month I wrote an essay about industry things to go along with the editorial and our ad.

Well, I left there, and I posted that TQ White is leaving the company. If you want to talk to him — and I put my contact information on it. They were pissed. The guy calls me up and says: "I can't believe you did that. We were going to give you severance, but now — screw you."

But I had a job literally the next day. A guy in Arizona that wanted to make a newspaper thing — the one I told you about. He hired me as a contractor to do the market research.

I knew everything about every aspect of the printing business, except for newspapers.
label: Early content marketing at Krauss America and departure

text:
## Newspaper Startup (~1997–1998)

**TQ:** Anyway, you went to work for this guy who was doing newspapers, which is one printing thing you didn't know about.

And so I networked my way into that. I ended up making friends with a guy at the Dallas Morning News. There's another one somewhere in Ohio or something. I negotiated a support deal for materials with Kodak. Again: what the hell was going on?

I designed his product and organized who and how we would sell it. At some point I'm doing technical design for the surrounding technology. He's now got a team of people starting to build. I get to design stuff.

I'm working on the data — the image data format. This is '97. TIFF has the characteristics of being lossless full-resolution data, and that was what we needed to use for communication within the system.

And one day I'm digging into TIFF, and I realize that TIFF is probably derived from my earlier file image interchange format. The way I know is because I made a fatal flaw in my thing. Any decent computer guy would build a format like that and have it be parametrically driven. In particular, the format I did had to specify the number of bytes per pixel, and I said: it's four. Instead of whatever the value in the header is. The TIFF image did that too. There's no way it would have been done if it had been an independent thing. There were a couple of other small clues, but that's the big one.

**Adam:** So they stole your IP, in other words, because you got a patent for that, right?

**TQ:** No, that was not a patented thing. The thing I got a patent for was a system that did color correction. And I shared it with the guy who actually knew about color correction.

Anyhow, I ended up getting kicked out of that. Fired is the reasonable thing, because I made a judgment about what to say to a magazine that this guy disagreed with. We ended up in a screaming match because he was so wrong and he was so certain that he was right.
label: Newspaper startup contract and firing (~1997–1998)

text:
## Lean Years / Website Consulting (~1998–2003)

**TQ:** And by this time I was doing a decent amount of website consulting. I was looking for a job, did an active job search, but it was not as energetic as it could be because I was too busy doing web stuff.

That went on for a few years and it was not good. I was not making enough money. I had a family. It was not good, but it worked. We were living.
label: Lean years and website consulting (~1998–2003)

text:
## Backup Bank / Restorion Startup (~2003–2004)

**TQ:** And in the 80s, I'd had an idea that I called Backup Bank — for remote backup databases. There was a particular consulting gig where they ended up losing their backups, and I dreamt of: if only we had the ability to have the backups get piped to a remote location automatically, it'd be so cool.

I told my wife at the time about it and she thought it was the best thing in the world and she named it Backup Bank. She was always kind of irritated, in a good-natured way, that I never pursued it.

At some point I got the idea to pursue it, and I did. And it failed. But my main measure of success is I managed to convince people to spend three quarters of a million dollars on it. I sold this idea.

I actually had, some years earlier, gotten it in my head that every enterprising young man ought to know how to write a business plan. I bought some books, I wrote business plans. I had done a few of them. And I did an actual formal business plan for Backup Bank, recruited money, and did it.

The first investor finally got cold feet. And I sold another one. And he got cold feet. I sold another one, but the second guy wouldn't cooperate.

It was another extraordinarily cool thing. And it should have worked. I blame the failure on a marketing company that was profligate with our money, and the money guy — it became apparent that we needed a critical mass of users. People didn't trust it and they weren't willing to pay. It was expensive — 35 bucks a month per computer. That was not out of line for the time.

**Adam:** But people weren't willing to commit to it.

**TQ:** And by this time, 2003 or 2004, the notion of "free to get started" had started to enter the zeitgeist. And I was like: we've got to do this.

**Adam:** And he wouldn't.

**TQ:** We ran out of money. He had spent as much as he was willing to spend. And I found another guy who wanted to do it. But he wanted to buy it. He was willing to let Bob keep 10% and like 10 grand. But he wanted to hire me. And Bob hated the idea that I got to continue. When he found out that the guy was planning on hiring me, he pulled out of the deal.

Which sucked. But that's the way it goes. It's a shame because people make a lot of money on backup now. And I did a good job.

We had a completely working system, all kinds of good stuff. One of my innovations was a variation on multilevel marketing that was not exploitive. I encouraged sales guys to build sales teams and gave them a cut of the action up through the tree.

It was a cool idea. I couldn't get it going because they couldn't give it away, so they couldn't get people to believe it would work.
label: Backup Bank / Restorion startup (~2003–2004)

text:
## Wilderness Years, SCW Fitness Gig, Best Buy Contract (~2004–2007)

**TQ:** That came undone and I spent a couple of years in the woods. I did a very active job search. One of my favorite things about that: I did a newsletter on my job search that I circulated to everybody I knew — friends, family, previous coworkers, everybody. I sent it to 30 or 40 people more often than once a month. It was a formatted, full thing. And I did cool web-oriented job applications for some of the bigger prospects.

I've never gotten a job from a job hunt except for once. And I didn't in this case. I did get a consulting gig that lasted for a couple of years that produced like 50 grand a year. So I stayed alive.

Getting through Restorion — which is what we ended up with as the final name of the company — I left that absolutely broke. Credit card debt out the ass. Along the way, my marriage ended and I moved up here.

I got a gig with SCW Fitness, a company that did training for fitness folk, run by a crazy woman. She was successful and cool. I went to their office and they had green-screen computers all over the place. And they have customer records open, with credit card numbers visible on the screen.

I made a very cool website for them that did enrollment and all of the online business stuff, including scheduling for their training conventions. She just got tired of paying. Basically she just got tired of paying.

I ended up one time being so broke that I couldn't afford gas to drive to Chicago to see my kids.

My resume at the time talked about all of the cool stuff that I do. And as you hear, it is a really impressive and varied bunch of stuff. And nobody wanted to hear it.

I end up talking to some headhunter lady. She says: "You know, I think I could sell you. But you got to get rid of all of this stuff and just be a programmer. Strip it down. If you get rid of all this other stuff, actively conceal the fact that you ever had a job that wasn't programming — I'm pretty sure I can get you a job."

And I've done enough marketing to know — oh, good idea. Duh. I hate that it's really boring, but it'll be a job. And I needed a job. Selling myself as Superman had not been working.

She never got me a job. But I ended up getting a contract job for a year working for Best Buy that caused me to spend two and a half months at some partner company of theirs in England. And that got me back into professional programming.
label: Wilderness years, SCW Fitness, and Best Buy contract (~2004–2007)

text:
## Hired at CMERDC (~2007)

**TQ:** That ended. And I'm looking for a job. And I got what turned out to be my last job, at the Central Minnesota Educational Research and Development Council. Which is how all this education stuff started happening.

The company was a technical services company for school districts. An old company started in the 60s. One of its earliest things was they ran the movie library for school districts — so teachers would order a movie for their classes, and these guys had the movie and brought it to them.

They got into school supplies and bulk order. And along the way the movie stuff morphed to video, which eventually morphed into some kind of technology attitude. They bought a data mining company, and my boss — a guy named Mike Schroeder — came along with that.

When I got there, it was maybe 15 people. Various programmers and a few tech support people. And I came to realize over the years that it was like an artist's atelier. It was absolutely not any kind of professional programming shop. It was a place where people did stuff — to some extent that Mike wanted to do, to some extent that they wanted to do.

They had a data warehousing thing. A special education case management system. Another thing called Transition — what you do for special needs people to get them out of school and into the workforce. And they had a report card production system.
label: Hired at CMERDC (~2007)

text:
## Express Book Report Card System Project at CMERDC

**TQ:** I got hired to write a cool new version of that report card system. A contract from the biggest school district in Minnesota — Anoka-Hennepin. Patrick Plant wanted a good report card system, grading system. He wanted a grade book that fed into report cards.

This was it. They hired me to do this project. They hadn't even had a contract yet. I helped them write the contract. Again: Mr. Versatile comes to the rescue. I made friends with the clients, did all the investigation, learned the requirements, wrote the specs, figured out all the stuff, prototyped it, and ran the project.

At the time, I was — honestly — a half-assed programmer in my view. But this brought me into modern techniques and where I have thrived and prospered since.

I did this thing and it was unbelievably cool. It was another career highlight. In its interesting complexity and appropriateness and it worked — people liked using it. We got it in and did the first year and things were going pretty well.

And then every single other person that was involved in the project left. Patrick Plant retired. The VP responsible left the company. The actual project manager, my day-to-day contact — he quit and got a new job. A collaborator at CMERDC, the guy who wrote the back-end C# to my front-end — he quit and went to Germany.

Leaving literally me as the only person that knew anything about it.

That meant the project was over. It was really, really sad. I held it together for half a year, but it was not good. It had the advantage that I learned to write C#, but I couldn't go anywhere. The client wasn't there.
label: Express Book report card system project at CMERDC

text:
## Data Warehouse Automation Work at CMERDC

**TQ:** Along the way, this data warehouse — it's a warehouse, it has to ingest data, and lots of it. The main data it ingested was test scores, assessments. We had 80 districts, tens of thousands of kids, and five or six assessments per kid happening ongoing. A lot of data.

They had a couple of people whose lives were dedicated to receiving this data and cranking it into the data warehouse. And one day I'm standing around Dan Davis's office — I'm still friends with him — and he's working, and I'm talking to him and watching what he's doing. And I'm basically just killing time because I didn't feel like working.

And I see what he's doing: really, you got to do that? I could automate that in a heartbeat, man. I can make it so that it grabs this file, copies it, puts it there, does these four steps for you. I could just do that, you wouldn't have to do all this knuckleheaded stuff.

And by this time I'm like five or six years into being there. I could do what I wanted. If I thought it was a good idea, Mike thought it was a good idea. I just did it.

A day or two later I show up with a tool. I install the program on his computer. Four or five fiddly little steps he had to do — like that, bingo.

I ended up spending the rest of my time there — another four years or something — with this being one of my big things. I entirely automated the whole process. By the time it was all done, it was: in dog, out sausage, all the way into the warehouse. Dan only had to debug stuff.

I equipped him with fabulous tools to solve problems. We were talking yesterday — I blabbered with him — about a thing I made called SQL Sequence, which was a small but powerful tool that he used like crazy.

I changed the latency of the data the teachers were seeing — when they're trying to figure out how their students are doing — from, on average, three weeks or more, to twice a day.

**Adam:** Wow.

**TQ:** When I left, it ran for another four years before they closed the company. And during that time, it had no programmer taking care of it. It worked for four years unattended. Dan could add new assessments and new school districts. I built an extremely cool tool. It was configuration-driven. Using configuration, he could bolt together a new school district that had previously unknown characteristics.
label: Data warehouse automation work at CMERDC

text:
## A4L Special Education Data Model Work

**TQ:** The last thing of that story: one of the things that happens along the way is Mike Schroeder — who has a profoundly disabled child and is as hardcore a bleeding-heart liberal as I am — gets it in his head that he wants to help the School Interoperability Framework have a special education data model.

I actually used SIF in Express Book — tried to use it at the very beginning of my time at CMERDC. It was silly. But I knew about it.

Mike says: "Do you think you could do this?" He loved me — not just liked me. He and I had an affectionate relationship. He was three weeks younger than I am. I kept telling him that he should listen to his older brother.

He hooks me up with A4L. I volunteered for a committee doing some other data thing to learn the ropes. Then I started my special education data model work group. I recruited a half-dozen companies to participate. Nobody really did anything except me, of course.

What I did, over the course of almost three years, is understand the business — understand special needs, learn about individualized education programs. An IEP is a federally mandated service that every school district has to provide to any special needs students they identify.

At some point I had talked to people all over the country. I googled up the IEP forms that states publish, all over the country. And I analyzed those things. Boiled it down to about ten of them that covered the gamut. Then spent — and God love Mike because he paid for this — almost three months boiling through these things, figuring out all of the data elements, all the structures, designing a data model. An elaborate thing.

Then reviewing it with people. Building it out in XML in a formal thing. Working with the CEDS — the Federal Standards Body — to get terms into the dictionary for it. Another unbelievable exercise.

In the meantime, I'm giving presentations at the annual conferences of both A4L and of CMERDC.

I made this thing happen. It exists and is vastly respected. There's not a person that's looked at it that hasn't praised it as a truly incredible piece of work.

**Adam:** Nobody was ever able to use it.

**TQ:** There's reasons for that. The biggest one being that the special needs industry — the first handicapped support law was passed in the mid-70s, IDEA. It incremented and accreted throughout the entire time from then till now. When people started using computers for it, it was so early they didn't know how to use computers. So they did terrible, terrible things. And this stuff never goes away. All of the case management systems — including ours, and we put a big amount of work into trying to use the data model — you couldn't do it. There was just no way to get there from here.

But it was good work and it moved the ball forward.
label: A4L special education data model work

text:
## White House Committee on IEP Data Portability

**TQ:** This jumps out of CMERDC, but now I am involved in something that started with Jill Biden. She came to understand that army brats with handicaps were getting screwed because their parents get moved around.

**Adam:** And the IEPs don't travel with them.

**TQ:** And she's like: we could fix that. She fired up a White House committee to do that. And they reached out to me. Because I am the world expert on IEP data.

**Adam:** In a lot of ways, more of an expert than the people that write the case management systems.

**TQ:** Because unlike them, I studied it and documented it. I have a body of work that can be used as a reference. That got me involved. And that has served as the basis for a project that is ongoing — to come up with a new version that is simpler, more narrowly targeted. And I am one of the leaders of that organization to this day.
label: White House committee on IEP data portability

text:
## Fired from CMERDC

**TQ:** So I end up actually getting fired outright from CMERDC. The guy who had been Mike's partner in crime decides he has to move to California. He was the boss of the company and supported Mike in his atelier all this time. He leaves. They have a woman take over for a year. She was no leader. She's kind of tight. She had one of her friends be the HR lady. She did not like me. And I did not like her.

They do an actual personnel hunt for a new leader. They hire a guy. Three weeks later, he fired me.

My boss, my direct report, was a woman named Sheila. She cried. She begged them not to do it. I was a central character in all kinds of stuff. They had a lot of problems with me gone.

But I was also the highest paid person in the company. And I think that's why he kicked me out. And everybody hated him in the ensuing months and thought he was a jackass. Mike's whole organization is entirely disbanded. The data warehouse company was sold for pennies on the dollar. And the company's now back to selling paper towels.
label: Fired from CMERDC
